Identity.History: Alexander the Great was king of Macedon from 336–323 BC and one of history’s most successful conquerors. Son of Philip II and student of Aristotle, he inherited a powerful army from his father and launched an invasion of the Persian Empire. He defeated Darius III at Granicus, Issus, and Gaugamela, conquering Anatolia, Egypt, Mesopotamia, Persia, and parts of Central Asia before campaigning into India. He founded cities, spread Greek culture, and encouraged fusion between Macedonian, Greek, Persian, and local elites. His sudden death in Babylon at age 32 left no stable heir, and his empire was divided among his generals.
